data.title: Amazon: Anthropic X Microsoft Deal Changes Nothing
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) in September, I said that AWS (Amazon Web Services) revenue should start to reaccelerate thanks to its golden goose Anthropic (ANTHRO) beginning pre-training of Claude 5 in Q4 this year and adding inference revenue on top.

Rightfully so, Amazon saw its AWS revenues accelerate to 20% YoY in Q3 FY25 earnings, its fastest growth rate in 11 quarters, which sent shares to an all-time high not too long ago.

Unfortunately, the latest weakness in the AI landscape has wiped out most of the post-earnings gain. On top of that, Microsoft (MSFT) announced a new strategic partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) and Anthropic yesterday that would see the two companies investing $5B and $10B, respectively, in the startup to support Anthropic’s future revenue growth.

While some investors could be concerned over the potential fading of the AWS tailwind as a result of the news, I explain why that is not the case. Especially as Anthropic sees its valuation balloon from $183B at the time of my last writing to $350B, along with clarifying that Amazon AWS will continue to remain their primary cloud and training provider. Not to mention, this is not a zero-sum game.

In the meantime, I will continue to reiterate my Buy rating on Amazon with an updated price target of $310 per share, representing an upside of 40% from its current levels.

AWS Accelerates Much Faster Than Anybody Was Expecting

Going into earnings, we were fairly confident that all three hyperscalers would exceed consensus estimates for their respective cloud growth rates, given that their backlog was already expanding.

Specifically, we were optimistic that Amazon AWS (Amazon Web Services) would start to see its revenue growth accelerate in Q3, thanks to Anthropic, where it is currently building three data center campuses, which will total 1.3 gigawatts of capacity and are expected to primarily utilize Amazon’s Trainium chips.

In my previous post, I had also pointed readers to the piece SemiAnalysis had written, where they believed that Amazon AWS should see its revenue accelerate to 18.5% in Q3 FY25 and will continue to accelerate until Q2 FY26, where growth will peak at 25.3%.

It turned out that Amazon AWS was able to accelerate its growth rate far higher than what consensus and SemiAnalysis had projected, growing their revenues 20% YoY to $33B, contributing 22% to Amazon’s total revenue. This was the fastest pace that the company grew at since 2022 and their largest growth rate in 11 quarters. Not only that, but the operating income from the AWS segment grew both on a sequential and year-over-year basis, contributing over 66% of the company’s total operating income.

During the earnings call, CEO Andy Jassy discussed that AWS has been aggressively building out its physical capacity to meet demand, adding more than 3.8 gigawatts of DC capacity in the past 12 months. Note that this is double the power capacity since 2022, with the company on track to double again in 2027, as they raised their capex projections for the full year by 6% to $125B.

On the topic of monetization, Amazon’s backlog grew to $200B in Q3, where they continued to onboard large customers and workloads, along with their Anthropic partnership that is translating directly into revenue, as Anthropic utilizes massive AWS compute clusters (like Project Rainier) to train and deploy its Claude models. On that note, Anthropic is expected to be on more than 1M Trainium2 chips by year-end, with Trainium revenues growing 150% QoQ as adoption deepens.

In all of this, I would like to point out that it is not just Amazon, but also Microsoft and Alphabet (GOOG) that also saw their cloud growth rates accelerate during the quarter that led them to revise their capex projections higher. However, for Amazon AWS, the revenue acceleration is more impressive in my opinion, given that it has the largest market share with a $132B annualized revenue run rate, which is roughly 1.5x and 3x the size of Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud Platform, respectively.

Take a Non-Zero-Sum Mindset Towards the Anthropic/Microsoft Partnership

One of the concepts we discussed in the previous post (borrowed from the SemiAnalysis post) is that of an “anchor customer” to determine forward hyperscaler performance. Think of how OpenAI is an “anchor customer” of Microsoft’s. Similarly, Amazon has invested $8B in Anthropic between 2023 and 2025, where Anthropic uses AWS as its primary cloud provider as well as its custom silicon Trainium and Inferentia to train and deploy its Claude model series.

During this period of time, Anthropic’s valuation has also exploded as it positioned its stronghold in “enterprise” use cases with revenue projected to grow from $9B in 2025 to $26B in 2026, while expecting to break even by 2027/2028, a very different story from OpenAI, which is projected to burn cash at a historic rate into 2029.

In fact, yesterday, both Microsoft and Nvidia announced that they would invest a combined $15B in Anthropic, bringing its total valuation to $350B, up from $183B since I last wrote in early September.

As part of the agreement, Microsoft and Nvidia will invest up to $5B and $10B, respectively, into Anthropic, which has committed to purchasing $30B of Azure compute capacity and has contracted for additional compute capacity up to 1 gigawatt with Nvidia’s Grace Blackwell and Vera Rubin systems.

In case you are thinking that Anthropic’s latest partnership with Microsoft and Nvidia will impact Amazon negatively, I will urge you to take a non-zero-sum mindset. Instead, think about how supporting Anthropic’s future revenue growth will help drive further innovation in the AI ecosystem and allow all investors with a stake to win simultaneously.

As Anthropic sees its valuation expand from faster-than-expected revenue growth, Amazon’s investment is already paying off financially. Second, despite the investments from Microsoft and Nvidia, Anthropic issued a statement clarifying that Amazon will continue to be their primary cloud and training provider. This means AWS along with Trainium2 adoption will continue to accelerate in the coming quarters, while the partnership with Microsoft and Nvidia will allow Anthropic to run specific workloads that are optimized for Nvidia clusters via Azure, especially as capacity remains constrained.

Raising My Price Target On Amazon

Following the Q3 earnings, consensus estimates have raised their forward expectations for both revenue and earnings for Amazon. On the revenue front, Amazon is now expected to grow its revenues close to 12% YoY in FY25 to $714.5B, while maintaining similar growth rates over the next 3-5 years. While we have already discussed the renewed tailwind from AWS, Amazon’s Stores business (North America and International segments) also accelerated, growing 11% and 14% YoY, respectively, across segments as the team continued to innovate across selection, prices, convenience, and delivery speeds.

At the same time, their AI-powered shopping assistant Rufus is also estimated to deliver over $10B in incremental annualized sales, as users utilizing the AI assistant during their shopping trips are 60% more likely to complete a purchase during a shopping trip. Finally, Amazon Ads continue to be a major area of strength within the Stores segment, which grew 22% YoY to $17.6B in revenue and contributed close to 10% of Total revenue.

When it comes to profitability, the higher capex will weigh down on profitability in the short term; however, investors are more focused on the accelerating backlog at this point. Post Q3, consensus has raised their forward earnings per share projections as well, as can be seen below, where earnings per share are expected to grow at a faster rate than revenue growth as a result of expanding contributions of AWS and Amazon Ads to total revenue.

At the moment, Amazon stock is currently trading at 31x its projected FY25 earnings per share. This is expected to contract to 23x over the next two years into FY27. During this period of time, earnings per share are expected to grow at 20%+ rate. As a result, I believe it deserves a premium of at least 2.2x that of the average S&P 500 price-to-earnings multiple, where the average company grows their earnings by 9% every year. This translates to a PE ratio of 40, or a price target per share of $310, which represents an upside of 40% from current levels.

Risks And Conclusions

While a 40% upside potential in the stock price is very attractive, note that the AI landscape is currently going through a correction, where investors have gotten skeptical after Michael Burry’s puts on Nvidia and Palantir along with tighter liquidity conditions amid growing lending in the AI landscape. Meanwhile, Amazon is still heavily reliant on its consumer business, which accounts for 78% of Total Revenue. Particularly, its North America Stores business contributes 60% of Total Revenue and could be subject to a slowdown if the overall macroeconomic environment worsens from a weakening labor market.

However, with Amazon as my second largest position in The Pragmatic Optimist Portfolio, I am confident in the long-term growth story, where I see it hugely benefitting from Anthropic’s expanding revenue growth as it trains and deploys its upcoming model series along with enterprise AI workloads, while its investments in robotics and automation pay off handsomely in gaining market share in e-commerce.

data.title: Powell Industries Q4: Decent Performance And Decent Outlook Next Year
data.description:
Introduction

Powell Industries (POWL) just reported its Q4 and full-year ’25 results, and given that the company is my largest holding, I wanted to go through the results in more detail and give some comments on what I think is in store for the company for 2026 and why I am still not worried about the AI growth story.

Last time I wrote about POWL was back in July, and since then, the company has almost doubled at one point, but has somewhat given up some of those gains due to overall market jitters surrounding the AI growth story. The company is still up 52% compared to the S&P500’s (SPY) 6% gain.

By the Numbers

Starting from the top, the company’s sales came in at $298m, up 8.4% y/y, beating estimates by around $5m. Looking into the company’s revenue segments in more detail for the full year performance, the primary drivers of growth were the Electric Utility and Commercial & Other Industrial sectors, which grew 50% and 19%, respectively. Additionally, the Light Rail Traction Power market saw an 87% increase in 2025. This performance was partially offset by some declines in the Oil & Gas and Petrochemical markets, which declined 3% and 19%, respectively, for the year.

For Q4, Electric Utility and Light Rail Traction Power revenues surged by 100% and 85%, respectively, but were partially offset by weakness in the mentioned Oil and Petrochemicals markets, which declined by 10% and 25%, respectively.

New orders came in at $1.2B, up 9% compared to the end of 2024. The growth can be attributed to higher activity across Oil & Gas, Electric utility, and Light Traction Power markets. Regional and end-market momentum remains the strongest in North America, with AI-driven electrification projects taking center stage.

In terms of backlog numbers, it saw a growth of around 3% compared to last year to $1.4B, and a decrease of 2% sequentially. The backlog continues to be slightly underwhelming with very little growth; however, it is better than when it was stagnating around $1.2B a few quarters ago, so that’s a win in my book.

Looking at the company’s proficiency and profitability, Q4 GAAP EPS came in at $4.22, beating analysts’ estimates by $0.46. A decent beat. Gross profit for Q4 came in at around $93.5m, which is 31.4% of sales vs 29.2% last year, a 220bps expansion. Operating margin came in at 21% vs 20.4% last year, a 60bps point expansion. Net income came in at $51.4m, an increase of 12%. The company has become a lot more profitable in just a year, which should be well-received by investors who were a bit more skeptical about the company’s operations in the past.

What about POWL’s financial position? The company ended the fiscal year with $475.5m in cash, equivalents, and short-term investments, against no debt as before, which puts the company in a fantastic position financially. The balance sheet is very strong, which allows it to pursue further growth through acquisitions or even rewarding shareholders through share buybacks or sustained dividends.

In terms of outlook, there hasn’t been much mentioned regarding next year. No concrete numbers provided, however, the company’s CFO, Michael Metcalf, is “pleased with” the company’s financial performance for the year, the margin profile, and the schedule of the backlog, and expects to deliver “another year of solid financial results in fiscal 2026”. He also notes the typical seasonality of the company’s first quarters, which means he expects a softer next quarter, but assures that on a total year basis, the margin profile achieved in 2025 is very much sustainable going forward, which is very good to hear.

This to me sounds like we are going to be in for another decent year just like 2025.

So overall, a beat on top and bottom lines was quite good to see. Initial reactions in the after-market action were positive. The shares at one point were up over 8%, but right now are more or less flat. It is no surprise since the company is very lightly traded as it is, even when the markets are open, so massive swings are expected from this company. I would say the improvements in margins are a good development, while the weakness in what I thought in the past were the main drivers of sales is a little disappointing, but electrification of all things is going to help pick up the slack going forward. The conference call is going to happen on Wednesday, 11:00 am EST, and I expect to hear more about the company’s outlook and further volatility in the share price. To sum up the quarter and the year, I am leaning towards it being a good year overall, with some mixed results in there, specifically, weakness in the Oil and petrochemicals markets and somewhat flat backlog.

Comments on the Outlook

The company clearly benefited from the ever-rising demand for the power of Artificial Intelligence. At one point, its shares traded as low as around $150 this year and recently shot up to an all-time high of $413. I have owned this name for a while now, and I am no stranger to massive fluctuations in the company’s shares, so I am not surprised that when it reached new highs, some people decided to take profits. I was one of those people. The company once again became a bit too big in my portfolio, and while examining my holdings, I noticed I was a bit too heavily invested in the AI growth story, which has worked out quite well; indeed, however, I trimmed my position in POWL slightly so that I can diversify a bit better. POWL remains my largest holding, and I will continue to ride this AI wave forward, no matter what happens.

Speaking of AI. The recent market jitters brought many companies down right now because many more start to believe that all this capex spending on AI is getting out of hand, with very little to show for it. Most of the hyperscalers have reported their numbers, and to no one’s surprise, all of them are looking to spend even more of their resources to keep this AI race alive. Alphabet (GOOG) and Meta (META) have been rather vague about 2026 capex spending on AI and data center infrastructure, but said that they are looking to spend a lot more than they did in 2025. These two companies alone will most likely account for around $200b in capex spending for 2026. And this is not even mentioning the other AI players like Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), and Microsoft (MSFT), who are not going to slow down either if they want to keep up. MSFT is looking to build an AI “superfactory,” and you know it is going to cost a lot of money and will require an absurd amount of power.

Some of this capex spending will continue to trickle down to POWL one way or another. AI and data centers have been a significant growth driver for the company, and it is very good to see that all these tech giants continue to shovel billions of dollars into this new technology, which seems to care little whether it is going to be profitable in the end or not. For now, I believe it is wise to just continue to ride the wave of the AI growth story. I trimmed my position slightly and will leave the rest alone.

On the other hand, some of these valuations are getting a bit frothy. I mentioned market jitters right now surrounding the heavyweights in the AI space, and now there are a lot more people who think that we are in an AI bubble and primed for a pop. I am worried about it too; however, I don’t think this is going to pop next year (famous last words), but if it does, no company will be safe from a pullback, even if a company has nothing to do with AI. I think some sort of decent pullback is healthy going forward.

The good thing about the company’s revenue is that it doesn’t solely rely on AI-related spending. Historically, the oil & gas segment has been a major revenue driver, accounting for a lot of the total revenues, as well as decent contributions from industrial and utility sectors, so while AI and data center electrification are growing end markets, Powell is decently diversified to absorb shocks in demand and I believe the cyclicality will turn positive in the underperforming markets like petrochemicals and oil & gas. I don’t think this will help the company’s share price from not being volatile, mainly because it is so lightly traded, so if AI somehow becomes unattractive, the shares will take a massive dive.

Closing Comments

As I mentioned earlier, I have trimmed my position slightly, but POWL remains my largest position right now with a very good average that is well below the current share price, so I am going to continue to ride the AI wave. If for some reason, the company’s share price returns to sub-$200, I will once again add a lot more, especially if nothing changes in terms of the company’s outlook. I can see this happening given the volatility, but I don’t think the AI growth story is over just yet.

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: Huge Dividend Surprise
data.description:
Article Thesis

Medical Properties Trust, Inc. (MPW) is a healthcare REIT that has had major problems in recent years, but the company seems to be progressing well with its turnaround. At the beginning of this week, we got the news that Medical Properties Trust's dividend will be raised by a huge 12.5% -- this is not only very positive for income investors, but it also indicates confidence in the company's progress in cleaning up its operations.

Past Coverage

I have written about Medical Properties Trust here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in April, or a little more than half a year ago, when I wrote this article. I gave the company a "Buy" rating back then, which hasn't worked out too well yet, as MPW has generated a positive return so far, but it wasn't able to perform as well as the broad market -- although I'm happy to see that the first dividend increase, which I had covered as part of my bullish thesis, has now been announced. With a little more than two quarters having passed since my last article on Medical Properties Trust, and with the company's big dividend news a couple of days ago, it is time for me to take another look at Medical Properties Trust today.

What Happened?

On Monday, Medical Properties Trust announced one of the biggest news items in the company's recent history:

A dividend increase by itself is already positive news, but the magnitude of the increase was, I think, a quite positive surprise for many investors: Double-digit dividend increases are usually what we see from high-growth companies with low dividend yields, not what we see from embattled real estate investment trusts. And yet, MPW announced just that -- its new dividend, at $0.36 per year, is $0.01 per quarter higher than the previous level, which Medical Properties Trust has held for the preceding five quarters, ever since it had cut the payout during the third quarter of 2024. It is important to note that this dividend increase doesn't offset the two dividend cuts we saw over the last year -- the dividend was considerably higher in 2022, when Medical Properties Trust paid out $0.29 per quarter. The company will have to increase its dividend by quite a lot if it ever wants to get back to that pre-crisis level. That being said, I think no one expects Medical Properties Trust to pay out dividends at a pre-crisis level anytime soon, otherwise the company's stock wouldn't trade at $5.10 today. Based on current prices, MPW's dividend yield is 7.1% right now, which is pretty nice, even in a no-growth or low-growth scenario. If Medical Properties Trust were to increase its dividend by just a third or half the rate of the just-announced increase going forward, or around 4% to 6% per year, that would make for an attractive yield + growth combination. Of course, there is no guarantee that the dividend will grow in the future -- but it would be a weird decision from the company's management team to announce one major dividend increase now if they did not believe that the company would be able to offer at least some dividend increases in the future.

I thus believe that this dividend increase is nice for income investors by itself, but that it is also a bit of a vote of confidence by management: The dividend increase makes sense if they see things improving going forward and if they believe that the worst is behind MPW. If management thought that the company is troubled, a dividend increase wouldn't make sense -- it would make more sense to reduce the dividend in such a scenario.

Medical Properties Trust: Turnaround Is Progressing Well

If MPW's management team believes that the worst is behind the company, that makes sense, I believe. The company's most recent quarterly earnings report indicates the same -- things are improving again. Medical Properties Trust's most recent earnings results, for the fiscal third quarter, show that revenues rose at a mid-single digit pace versus one year earlier, despite asset sales in recent years. The revenue increase of $12 million from one year earlier was almost entirely the result of higher rent billed, which rose by a little more than $11 million, while income from financing leases and interest income both improved slightly. The higher rent billed level is largely the result of the ramp-up of payments from the "new tenants" in Medical Properties Trust's properties that have taken over the properties where Stewart Health Systems had previously been the tenant. In Q4 and throughout 2026, cash payments from these tenants will increase further, to around $40 million per quarter, from a level of $16 million in the most recent quarter. Revenues should thus grow nicely next year, too, unless Medical Properties Trust does a major asset sale, which does not seem overly likely. And even if that were to happen, proceeds could be used to pay down debt, which wouldn't be a bad thing, either. In the Q3 earnings release, management also talks about the deal with NOR Healthcare Systems that will add $45 million in annual cash rent starting in 2026, thus revenues will get an additional boost on top of the positive impact from the Stewart Health Systems replacement tenants ramp-up.

Of course, revenue isn't the only important metric. For REITs, funds from operations are the most relevant profit metric, and here, Medical Properties earned $0.15 per share during the third quarter, of $0.60 annualized -- a pretty good result for a company with a share price of $5.10. Normalized funds from operations, which accounts for non-cash fair value adjustments, tax rate changes, etc., came in at $0.13 per share for the quarter, a little lower than FFO, but still at a level of $0.52 per year -- easily covering the dividend, and resulting in a normalized FFO multiple of just below 10, which is a rather low valuation.

With revenues forecasted to grow nicely next year, thanks to the ramp-up of payments from the aforementioned new tenants and rent escalators on existing tenants, Medical Properties Trust is expected to see its funds from operations grow at an attractive pace, too. This year's FFO per share estimate is $0.56, with that number being seen growing at a little more than 20%, to $0.68 next year. There is, of course, no guarantee that this estimate will be hit, but with management deciding on a big dividend increase this quarter, it would not surprise me if management thinks that a big profit improvement is coming next year.

Medical Properties Trust also recently announced a share repurchase program of $150 million, equal to around 5% of the company's current market capitalization. Using this buyback authorization as soon as possible looks like a good idea -- reducing the share count while shares trade at just 7x to 8x next year's expected funds from operations could create significant value for shareholders in the future, assuming the turnaround is successful. While debt levels are still high, which means debt reduction wouldn't be a bad thing at all, either, I still like that management believes shares are undervalued and that they want to use this opportunity.

Is MPW A Good Investment?

Medical Properties Trust has had a lot of problems in the past, but things are improving -- while far from a risk-free investment, MPW is seeing improving revenue trends, and 2026 should see significant additional cash rent coming in. With the new buyback program and the 12.5% dividend increase, management shows confidence, which is, I believe, a positive sign.

Medical Properties Trust offers a dividend yield of just above 7% and trades at only 7.5x next year's expected funds from operations -- if things go well, that could result in highly compelling total returns going forward.
